result:
The word “freely” arrives like a swinging door at the edge of a wheat field. I step through and the hinges don’t creak; they sigh, the way a library sighs when someone finally checks out the book that has waited thirty years to be touched. On the other side, the sky is the color of a voicemail you’re afraid to play. Clouds scroll across it like closed captions for a conversation the wind is having with itself. I’m barefoot, because shoes are just small prisons we agree to wear, and the soil remembers every foot that has ever pressed it—Roman sandals, buffalo hooves, the bare calloused pads of a kid who ran away to join a circus that turned out to be three trailers and a dream shrinking in the rain.

I keep walking until I find the vending machine someone abandoned here decades ago. It still hums, powered by nothing anyone can name. The glass is fogged except for one neat square wiped clean, and inside sits a single can labeled “Possible.” No brand, no nutrition facts, just that word in Helvetica Light. It costs nothing, but you have to insert a regret. I fish one out of my pocket: the time I laughed at a joke I didn’t understand because I wanted to belong. The machine swallows it with a soft mechanical purr, as if savoring the taste. The can drops. When I crack it open, there’s no hiss, just the sound of a piano chord from a song I loved before language. I drink. It tastes like the moment you realize your parents are people. Carbonated wonder, slightly chilled.

Ahead, the field tilts upward like a page being turned. I climb until the wheat ends abruptly at a cliff made of old cassette tapes. Their brown ribbons flutter in the breeze, hundreds of thousand of minutes of mixed playlists and recorded silences. One strand brushes my cheek and whispers, “Side B was always braver.” I believe it. At the edge, I look down and see an ocean of desk chairs arranged in neat rows, all facing the horizon. Each chair holds a single glowstick that has just begun to fade. They look like lighthouses embarrassed to admit they’re lost. I wave; no one waves back, but one chair spins slowly, a gesture that might be applause or farewell.

Instead of jumping, I lie on my back between two stalks of wheat that have twisted themselves into a loose Möbius. Above me, the sky reloads. Stars pop in like thumbnails buffering, each one a different year: 1994, 2001, 2039. I click on 2039 and it expands—soundless footage of me standing in a kitchen I don’t yet own, watering a basil plant that refuses to die. I look happy in the careless way glass looks happy when sunlight passes through. I whisper “keep going” to the future version of me, not as advice but as a vow. The clip freezes, buffers, collapses back into a pinpoint.

Somewhere behind me, the vending machine sneezes. I turn, but it’s already gone, replaced by a doorframe standing alone, no walls, no house, just the outline of passage. From the empty center, the smell of freshly sharpened pencils drifts out, and I understand it’s offering me a way back to the page where all of this began. But the wheat is taller now, singing in green harmonics about staying. I realize I don’t have to choose; I can be the hinge itself, neither here nor there but the motion between. So I take one step toward the frame and one step into the field, splitting myself like light through a prism. One beam keeps walking into tomorrow, the other settles into the ground, a seed that will grow into a different telling.

Both versions wave, not goodbye, but hello to everything that keeps arriving.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want.
